concepts.title: Characteristics of P-Block
concepts.conceptBody:
P-Block Elements

Introduction:

The right side of the periodic table having group number 13, 14, 15, 16, 17 and 18 are known as p-block elements. These elements have 3,4,5,6,7and 8 electrons in their outer most shell, respectively. The last electron of these groups' elements occupies the position in p-sub shell that is why they are called as$\text{p}$-block elements. Their general configuration is$n{{s}^{2}}n{{p}^{1-6}}$.

Some Important Properties of P-Block

1. Electron affinity:

Electron affinity increase from left to right along the period amongst the p-block elements and it decreases from top to bottom. But group 15 is having exceptionally low values of electron affinity and is due to extra stability because of the presence of exactly half filled orbital in their valence shell Similarly, elements of group 18 (noble gases) have zero affinities due to presence of complete octet which provides them stability.

2. Metallic Character

The metallic character is governed by

(i) Size of atoms and

(ii) Ionization energy.

The elements having bigger size and low ionization energy has a greater metallic character. After combining both above mentioned factors we observe that the elements with above two properties are located in left corner of p-block and strong non-metallic elements are located at right corner and a diagonal strip of elements thus two, having in between properties are called as metalloids.

3. Oxidation state

The p-block elements shows variety of oxidation states both positive and negative. Some of the p-block elements show different oxidation state due to inert - pair effect, where the lower oxidation state is more predominant.

4. Diagonal relationship

On moving diagonally across the periodic table the element show certain similarities.

Note: Elements of 2 nd period differ from their own group elements in some of the properties. This is due to the following reasons:

(a) Small size

(b) Absence of vacant d-orbital

(c) High IP

Some Important Characteristics of P-Block In Tabular Form

concepts.title: General Trends in Physical properties
concepts.conceptBody:
General Trends in Physical Properties

1. Density

Generally increases down the group but aluminium has an exceptionally low density.

2. Melting point and Boiling point

$\text{B}$ to $\text{Ga}$ decrease then $\text{Ga}$ to $\text{TI}$ increase Ga has lowest M.P. $(29.8\text{C})$ and therefore liquid at room temperature.

3. Atomic radii and ionic radii

On moving from $\text{B}$ to TI the size increases due to addition of new energy shells at each step down the group but $\text{Ga}$ is smaller than $\text{Al}$.

4. Ionization energy

Generally IE decreases down the group but Ga has higher IE than Al exceptionally due to smaller in size as compared to $\text{Al}$.

5. Metallic character

Electropositive character increases down the group hence metallic character also increase down the group but aluminium is having high metallic character than Gallium due to low IE than Ga.

6. Inter Pair Effect

When we go down the group in $p$ block element, stable positive valency decreases by a factor of 2. This effect is known as inert pair effect. It arises mainly due to poor shielding effect $3~\text{d}$ and $4~\text{d}$ series element. This result into increase in effective nuclear charge and greater hold on valence S electrons by nucleus. These s electrons can't be pulled easily out which decreases the valency by a factor of 2 .

e.g. In 14th group while carbon & silicon show +4 valency, while $\text{Pb}$ is mostly stable in +2 state.

concepts.title: Nature of Bonds
concepts.conceptBody:
7. Oxidation states and nature of bond

The group - 13 elements show variable oxidation states of +3 and +1 . For lighter members + 3 state is more stable than +1 . Thus for $\text{Tl},+1$ state is more stable than +3 . It is due to inert pair effect. The $6{{s}^{2}}$ pair does not prefer to form bonds and is called inert pair. The basic reason for such an effect is bond energy. The $\text{M}-\text{Cl}$ bond energy in $\text{MC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ compounds are as follows:

The ionisation energies of $B$ is very high. It is, therefore, covalent in its compounds, always tri-covalent $\left( 2~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2{{\text{p}}^{1}} \right.$ electrons).

In +3 oxidation state $\text{Al}$ has very high charge to radius ratio $(\sim6.0)$. So, it is also covalent in its anhydrous compounds. However, in aq. solution tri-covalent $\text{Al}$ (e.g. $\text{AlC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ ) becomes ionic due to high hydration energy of $\text{A}{{\text{l}}^{+3}}$ (4665 KJ mol$^{-1}$ ) ${{\left[ \text{Al}{{\left( \text{O}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)}_{6}} \right]}^{3+}}.3\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$. The soluble compounds are quite acidic due to hydrolysis (high charge to radius ratio of $\text{A}{{\text{l}}^{+3}}$ ).

${{\left[ \text{Al}{{\left( \text{O}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)}_{6}} \right]}^{+3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}+{{\left[ \text{Al}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{5}}\text{OH} \right]}^{+2}}$

TI is ionic in +1 oxidation state, TICl ionic. The$\text{T}{{\text{I}}^{+}}$ion resembles ${{\text{K}}^{+}}$and $\text{R}{{\text{b}}^{+}}$. It is because radii of the ions are comparable $\left( {{\text{K}}^{+}}\sim1.38{Å} \right.$, $\left. \text{TI}\sim1.5{Å},\text{Rb}\sim1.52{Å} \right)$. Therefore, TIOH is a strong base like group -1 hydroxides. Tri-covalent compounds, $\text{M}{{\text{X}}_{3}}$, are electron deficient as octet around the central atom is not complete.

Such compounds remove their electron deficiency by:

(i) Back - bonding 

In $\text{B}{{\text{X}}_{3}},~\text{B}$ is $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}$ and has a vacant p-orbital perpendicular at the triangular plane. $\text{X}$-atom has filled p-orbital. The overlap resulting into $\pi $-bonding.

(ii) Coordination with Lewis bases 

B has vacant orbital which accepts electron pair from ${{\text{F}}^{-}}$.

$Al{{(OH)}_{3}}+H{{O}^{-}}\to Al(OH)_{4}^{-}$

(iii) Polymerisation 

$\text{AlC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ removes electron deficiency by dimerisation.

$2\text{AlCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{6}}$

$\text{ Dimer is made easily because Cl has lp and Al has vacant orbital}\text{. }$

$\text{AlC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}.$is not stabilized by back bonding. It is due to the following facts:

(i) p-orbital of$\text{Al}$ is large, so $\text{p}-\text{p}$ overlap is not effective.

(ii) 8 electron core in $\text{Al}$ causes larger repulsion $(8\text{e}-8\text{e})$ destabilizing back bonding effective.

$\text{B}{{\text{X}}_{3}}$ can not stabilize by dimerisation. $\text{B}$ is small in size. The dimer will have small ring.

So, it is not stable.

concepts.title: Reaction of Elements of group 13
concepts.conceptBody:
Reactions Of Elements

Pure crystalline boron is very unreactive. However, it is attacked at high temperatures by strong oxidising agents such as a mixture of hot concentrated ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ and $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ or $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$.

$2~\text{B}(~\text{s})+6\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(\text{aq})\xrightarrow{{}}2{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(\text{aq})+6\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

In contrast, finely divided amorphous boron (containing about 2-5% impurities) is more reactive. Reactions are summarised in Table below

Reactions of Group 13 (IIIA) Elements

  • Boron reacts directly with elements forming borides which are hard and refractory.

$3\text{Mg}+2~\text{B}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{M}{{\text{g}}_{3}}~{{\text{B}}_{2}}$

Borides are decomposed by acids forming boranes

$\text{M}{{\text{g}}_{3}}~{{\text{B}}_{2}}+6\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}3\text{MgC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\underset{\text{diborane}}{\mathop{{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{6}}}}\,$

  • Aluminium is far more reactive than boron but in air and water it is stable. It is due to the formation of aluminium oxide, $\text{A}{{\text{l}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ layer, that makes it passive for further attack. $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ also makes $\text{Al}$ passive by oxide layer formation.
  • Reaction of $\text{Al}$ with ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is exothermic and is called thermite reaction.

$2\text{Al}(\text{s})+\frac{3}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}{{\text{l}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(~\text{s}),\quad \Delta \text{H}=-1670~\text{kJ}$

The thermite reaction involves so much energy that it can be dangerous. The $\text{Al}$ becomes white hot and often causes fires. For this precise reason, mixture of $\text{Al}$ and an oxide such as $\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ or $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ (a source of oxygen) were used to make incendiary bombs during World War II.

concepts.title: Borax
concepts.conceptBody:
1. Borax $\left( \mathbf{N}{{\mathbf{a}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{~}{{\mathbf{B}}_{\mathbf{4}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{7}}}\mathbf{.10}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{O} \right)$ 

  • The actual formula is $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\left[ ~{{\text{B}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}{{(\text{OH})}_{4}} \right]\cdot 8{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

  • Action of heat

  • Various reactions of borax

  • Solution of Borax is alkaline in nature. This is due to hydrolysis

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~{{\text{B}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}+7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \underset{\begin{smallmatrix} \text{ Strong } \\ \text{ alkali } \end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{2\text{NaOH}}}\,+\underset{\begin{smallmatrix} \text{ Weak } \\ \text{ acid } \end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{4{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}}\,$

concepts.title: Boric acids and Oxides
concepts.conceptBody:
2. Boric acids and oxides

(i) ${{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ or $\text{B}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}}$ orthoboric acid

(ii) $\text{HB}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ metaboric acid

(iii) ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~{{\text{B}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ pyroboric acid

${{H}_{3}}B{{O}_{3}}\xrightarrow[{}]{100{}^\circ C}HB{{O}_{2}}\xrightarrow[{}]{140{}^\circ C}{{H}_{2}}{{B}_{4}}{{O}_{7}}\xrightarrow[{}]{red\,\,hot}{{B}_{2}}{{O}_{3}}$

3. Boron Trioxide, ${{\mathbf{B}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$

• $2{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

It is a white hygroscopic solid. It absorbs moisture and converted into boric acid.

${{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{HB}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$\text{HB}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

• $2~{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{P}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{10}}\xrightarrow{{}}4\text{BP}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

• It is reduced by magnesium

${{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3\text{Mg}\xrightarrow{Heated}2~\text{B}+3\text{MgO}$

concepts.title: Similarity between Boron and Silicon
concepts.conceptBody:
Similarity between Boron and Silicon (Diagonal Relationship)

  • Both form volatile hydrides (called boranes and silanes) which spontaneously catch fire on exposure to air and are easily hydrolysed.
  • Halides of both (except$\text{B}{{\text{F}}_{3}}$) are easily hydrolysed:

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{BC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3\text{HCl} \\ \text{SiC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+4\text{HCl} \\ \end{array}$

  • Boron forms binary compounds with several metals-these are called borides. Silicon form silicides. Borides and silicides are hydrolysed by ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ forming boranes and silanes:

$\text{M}{{\text{g}}_{3}}~{{\text{B}}_{2}}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}3\text{Mg}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{6}}$

$\text{A}{{\text{l}}_{4}}\text{S}{{\text{i}}_{3}}+12{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}4\text{Al}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}}+3\text{Si}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$

  • ${{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ and $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ are acidic in nature and are important constituents of glass. Borate and silicates have tetrahedral $\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ and $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ structural units, respectively.
  • Both are semi-conductors.
  • Boric acid $\left( {{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)$ and silicic acids $\left( {{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)$ are weak crystalline.
  • They do not dissolve in cold dilute acids but do so in alkalies-boron only in fused alkalies and silicon in both fused and aqueous alkalies.

$2~\text{B}+2\text{NaOH}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{NaB}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{ }$

$\text{Si}+2\text{NaOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}$
